g.data-narration: A Process Identifier (PID) is a unique number assigned by the OS kernel to each running process.
g.text: Each process gets a unique positive integer.
Used by OS to track and manage processes.
Like a fingerprint for each running program.

g.data-narration: Without unique PIDs, the OS cannot distinguish between processes, causing chaos in scheduling and memory management.
The Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure that stores all information about a process, indexed by its PID.
PID 0 is reserved for the swapper/idle process, and PID 1 is reserved for init - the first user process.
Every process (except init) has a Parent Process. The PPID stores the parent's PID, creating a process tree.
g.text: PPID = PID of the parent process that created this process

g.data-narration: UID and GID determine process privileges. Real UID is the actual user, effective UID is used for permission checks.
g.text: System calls: getuid(), getgid(), geteuid(), getegid()

g.data-narration: The fork() system call creates a new process. The child gets a new PID while inheriting the parent's PPID.
A zombie process has terminated but its PCB remains because the parent hasn't called wait() to collect exit status.
An orphan process loses its parent when the parent terminates first. Init (PID 1) adopts orphaned children.
Daemon processes run in the background with PPID=1. They provide system services and don't have a controlling terminal.
Here's a practical C program demonstrating fork() and how parent-child processes retrieve their PIDs and PPIDs.
Key points: PIDs are unique integers, PCB stores process info indexed by PID, PPID creates hierarchy, fork() creates new processes.
g.text: ✓ PID = Unique identifier • PPID = Parent's PID • UID/GID = User/Group permissions
✓ PCB indexed by PID • fork() returns 0 (child), child's PID (parent) • wait() prevents zombies
✓ init = PID 1 • Orphans adopted by init

g.data-narration: Quick reference: PID 0 = swapper, PID 1 = init, zombie = dead + uncollected, orphan = parentless, daemon = background service.
